questions.correct_answer: '==' checks value only; '===' checks value and type

questions.question: What is a deadlock in operating systems?
questions.correct_answer: A state where two or more processes each wait for the other to release a resource
incorrect_answers: A program that crashes due to a null pointer
An infinite loop with no exit condition
A memory leak in a process

questions.question: What is polymorphism in OOP?
questions.correct_answer: The ability of different objects to respond to the same interface in different ways
incorrect_answers: The process of hiding internal implementation
A mechanism for a class to inherit from another
The practice of writing code once and reusing it

questions.question: What is inheritance in OOP?
questions.correct_answer: A mechanism where one class acquires properties and methods from another
incorrect_answers: Bundling data and methods into a single unit
The ability to redefine a method in a subclass
Creating multiple instances of a class

questions.question: What is the difference between the stack and the heap in memory?
questions.correct_answer: Stack is used for static allocation; heap is used for dynamic allocation
incorrect_answers: Heap is used for function call frames
Stack stores global variables; heap stores local ones

questions.question: What is Big O notation used for?
questions.correct_answer: Describing the time or space complexity of an algorithm in terms of input size
incorrect_answers: Measuring actual execution time in milliseconds
Comparing memory usage between two computers

questions.question: What is a linked list?
questions.correct_answer: A data structure where each element contains data and a pointer to the next element
incorrect_answers: A sorted collection stored in contiguous memory

questions.question: What is the purpose of normalization in relational databases?
questions.correct_answer: To reduce data redundancy and improve data integrity

questions.question: What does ACID stand for in the context of databases?
questions.correct_answer: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability

questions.question: What is a foreign key in a relational database?
questions.correct_answer: A field that references the primary key of another table
incorrect_answers: A unique identifier for each row

questions.question: What is the key difference between TCP and UDP?
questions.correct_answer: TCP is reliable and connection-oriented; UDP is faster but unreliable
incorrect_answers: UDP guarantees delivery; TCP does not
TCP is only used for video streaming

questions.question: What does MVC stand for in software architecture?
questions.correct_answer: Model-View-Controller

questions.question: What is the average-case time complexity of quicksort?
questions.correct_answer: O(n log n)

questions.question: What is encapsulation in OOP?
questions.correct_answer: Bundling data and the methods that operate on it into a single unit and restricting direct access
incorrect_answers: Allowing a class to inherit from multiple parents
Creating multiple objects from one class

questions.question: What is a pointer in C?
questions.correct_answer: A variable that stores the memory address of another variable
incorrect_answers: A variable that stores a boolean value
A reference to a function return type

questions.question: What is the purpose of a garbage collector?
questions.correct_answer: To automatically reclaim memory that is no longer in use
incorrect_answers: To compress unused files on disk

questions.question: What is a race condition?
questions.correct_answer: A bug where the outcome depends on unpredictable timing of concurrent operations
incorrect_answers: A performance bottleneck in the CPU
An infinite loop caused by a bad condition

questions.question: What is the difference between a process and a thread?
questions.correct_answer: A process is an independent program in execution; a thread is a lighter unit of execution within a process
incorrect_answers: A thread owns its own memory; a process shares memory
They are the same thing in modern operating systems
A process is faster than a thread

questions.question: What does HTTP status code 404 mean?
questions.correct_answer: Not Found — the requested resource does not exist

questions.question: What is a closure in programming?
questions.correct_answer: A function that retains access to variables from its enclosing scope even after that scope has exited
incorrect_answers: A function with no return value
A function that cannot be called recursively
An anonymous function with no parameters

questions.question: What is memoization?
questions.correct_answer: Caching the results of expensive function calls so repeated calls return the cached result

questions.question: What is the Singleton design pattern?
questions.correct_answer: A pattern that ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global access point to it
incorrect_answers: A pattern that creates many instances of an object
A pattern for chaining method calls
A pattern that decouples object creation from usage

questions.question: What is virtual memory?
questions.correct_answer: A memory management technique that uses disk space to simulate additional RAM

questions.question: What is the difference between a compiled and an interpreted language?
questions.correct_answer: Compiled languages are translated entirely to machine code before running; interpreted ones are translated line by line at runtime
incorrect_answers: Compiled languages run slower than interpreted ones
Compiled languages cannot be used on multiple platforms

questions.question: What is a regular expression?
questions.correct_answer: A sequence of characters that defines a pattern for searching or matching text
incorrect_answers: A mathematical formula used in algorithms

questions.question: What is method overloading?
questions.correct_answer: Defining multiple methods with the same name but different parameter lists
incorrect_answers: Redefining a parent class method in a subclass
Calling a method from inside itself
Assigning a method to a variable

questions.question: What does the CAP theorem state for distributed systems?
questions.correct_answer: A distributed system can guarantee at most two of: Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance
incorrect_answers: All distributed systems must be consistent and available
Consistency and availability are mutually exclusive

questions.question: What is the purpose of an index in a database?
questions.correct_answer: To speed up data retrieval by allowing faster lookups
incorrect_answers: To enforce unique constraints on a table

questions.question: What is a callback function?
questions.correct_answer: A function passed as an argument to another function and invoked at a later time
incorrect_answers: A function that returns another function
A function that runs before main
A function that only runs on errors

questions.question: What does CORS stand for in web development?
questions.correct_answer: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing

questions.question: What is dependency injection?
questions.correct_answer: Providing a class's dependencies from outside rather than having the class create them
incorrect_answers: Injecting malicious code into a dependency

questions.question: What is a lambda function?
questions.correct_answer: A small anonymous function defined inline
incorrect_answers: A function that returns multiple values
A function stored in a database

questions.question: What is the purpose of 'try-catch' blocks?
questions.correct_answer: To handle exceptions gracefully and prevent program crashes
incorrect_answers: To speed up execution by skipping validation
To test two code paths simultaneously

questions.question: What does CRUD stand for and what SQL operation maps to 'Create'?
questions.correct_answer: CRUD is Create, Read, Update, Delete; 'Create' maps to INSERT
incorrect_answers: CRUD is Copy, Retrieve, Update, Delete; 'Create' maps to ADD
CRUD is Create, Read, Update, Drop; 'Create' maps to NEW
CRUD is Copy, Read, Undo, Delete; 'Create' maps to PUT

questions.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of merge sort?
questions.correct_answer: O(n log n)

questions.question: What does DFS stand for in graph algorithms?
questions.correct_answer: Depth-First Search

questions.question: What is the difference between DFS and BFS in graph traversal?
questions.correct_answer: DFS explores as deep as possible before backtracking; BFS explores all neighbors level by level
incorrect_answers: DFS is always faster than BFS
BFS uses a stack; DFS uses a queue
They produce identical results on all graphs

questions.question: What is a promise in JavaScript?
questions.correct_answer: An object that represents the eventual completion or failure of an asynchronous operation
incorrect_answers: A guarantee that a function will not throw an error

questions.question: What is immutability in programming?
questions.correct_answer: The property where an object's state cannot be modified after it is created
incorrect_answers: The ability of a variable to hold any type
A data structure that cannot be read
A function that always returns the same type

questions.question: What is a context switch in operating systems?
questions.correct_answer: The process of saving and restoring a process's state so the CPU can switch between processes
incorrect_answers: Switching between user and kernel mode
Transferring data between memory and disk

questions.question: What is a mutex?
questions.correct_answer: A synchronization primitive that prevents multiple threads from accessing a shared resource simultaneously
incorrect_answers: A method that mutates an object

questions.question: What is SQL injection?
questions.correct_answer: An attack where malicious SQL code is inserted into an input to manipulate a database
incorrect_answers: A technique to speed up SQL queries
A method to back up a database
A way to connect two databases

questions.question: What is the purpose of load balancing?
questions.correct_answer: To distribute incoming network traffic across multiple servers to ensure no single server is overwhelmed
incorrect_answers: To compress data before sending it
To encrypt traffic between client and server
To cache static content near users

questions.question: What is XSS (Cross-Site Scripting)?
questions.correct_answer: An attack where malicious scripts are injected into trusted websites and executed in a victim's browser
incorrect_answers: A technique for secure data sharing between sites
A network protocol for cross-domain requests

questions.question: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous programming?
questions.correct_answer: Synchronous code blocks execution until complete; asynchronous code allows other operations to run while waiting
incorrect_answers: Synchronous code runs on multiple threads
They are two terms for the same concept

questions.question: What is an enum in programming?
questions.correct_answer: A type with a fixed set of named constant values
incorrect_answers: A function that enumerates list items
A mapping of strings to integers

questions.question: What is duck typing?
questions.correct_answer: A principle where an object's suitability is determined by its methods and properties rather than its type
incorrect_answers: A debugging technique using print statements
A form of strong static typing

questions.question: What is event-driven programming?
questions.correct_answer: A paradigm where the program flow is determined by events such as user actions or messages
incorrect_answers: Programming that runs on a fixed timer
A style that avoids using functions

questions.question: What is a decorator in Python?
questions.correct_answer: A function that wraps another function to modify or extend its behavior without changing its source code
incorrect_answers: A comment block above a class
A type annotation for a variable

questions.question: What is a REST API?
questions.correct_answer: An API that uses HTTP and follows Representational State Transfer constraints
incorrect_answers: An API that only works over WebSockets
An API stored entirely in a database
An API that requires authentication tokens only

questions.question: What is a binary tree?
questions.correct_answer: A tree data structure where each node has at most two children
incorrect_answers: A tree stored as binary data on disk
A tree with exactly two levels
A sorted list of binary numbers

questions.question: What is the purpose of the SSL/TLS protocol?
questions.correct_answer: To encrypt data transmitted over a network, ensuring confidentiality and integrity

questions.question: What is a microservices architecture?
questions.correct_answer: An approach where an application is built as a collection of small, independently deployable services
incorrect_answers: An architecture with a single shared database for all services
A design pattern for mobile apps
A monolithic application split across multiple files

questions.question: What does a CDN (Content Delivery Network) do?
questions.correct_answer: Delivers web content from servers geographically closer to the user to reduce latency
incorrect_answers: Encrypts data stored in the cloud
Manages DNS records for a domain

questions.question: What does 'git rebase' do?
questions.correct_answer: Moves or applies commits from one branch onto another base commit
incorrect_answers: Merges two branches into a single commit

questions.question: What is the purpose of Docker?
questions.correct_answer: To package applications and their dependencies into portable containers
incorrect_answers: To manage version control for code

questions.question: What is tail recursion?
questions.correct_answer: A form of recursion where the recursive call is the very last operation in the function, allowing stack optimization
incorrect_answers: A recursion that always reaches its base case immediately
Recursion that starts from the end of a list

questions.question: What is continuous integration (CI)?
questions.correct_answer: The practice of frequently merging developer code changes and running automated tests to detect issues early
incorrect_answers: Manually deploying code to production every week
A workflow where one developer writes all code

questions.question: What is a JWT (JSON Web Token) used for?
questions.correct_answer: Securely transmitting information between parties, commonly used for authentication
incorrect_answers: Storing user data in the browser's local storage

questions.question: What is the difference between '==' and 'is' in Python?
questions.correct_answer: '==' checks if two values are equal; 'is' checks if they are the same object in memory
incorrect_answers: 'is' is used for string comparison only

questions.question: What is a doubly linked list?
questions.correct_answer: A linked list where each node has pointers to both the next and previous nodes
incorrect_answers: A linked list with two separate heads
A linked list stored in two arrays
A linked list that loops back to the start

questions.question: What is the purpose of code refactoring?
questions.correct_answer: Restructuring existing code to improve readability and maintainability without changing its external behavior
incorrect_answers: Adding new features to existing code
Rewriting an application in a different language

questions.question: What is a semaphore in operating systems?
questions.correct_answer: A variable used to control access to a shared resource by multiple processes or threads
incorrect_answers: A signal sent between two computers

questions.question: What is a buffer overflow?
questions.correct_answer: A vulnerability where more data is written to a buffer than it can hold, overwriting adjacent memory
incorrect_answers: A network error caused by too many connections
A database error from too many rows
A UI bug caused by too much text

questions.question: What does an ORM (Object-Relational Mapper) do?
questions.correct_answer: Maps database tables and rows to programming language objects, allowing database interaction without raw SQL
incorrect_answers: Generates database schemas from JSON files

questions.question: What is the Observer design pattern?
questions.correct_answer: A pattern where an object maintains a list of dependents and notifies them automatically of state changes
incorrect_answers: A pattern that logs all function calls
A pattern for creating a single shared instance
A pattern that caches expensive computations

questions.question: What is the difference between a deep copy and a shallow copy?
questions.correct_answer: A deep copy recursively copies all nested objects; a shallow copy copies only references to nested objects
incorrect_answers: A shallow copy is always slower
A deep copy and shallow copy are identical for primitive types and objects
A shallow copy creates a new object with all new data

questions.question: What is Dijkstra's algorithm used for?
questions.correct_answer: Finding the shortest path between nodes in a weighted graph
incorrect_answers: Searching for a value in a binary tree
Detecting cycles in a directed graph

questions.question: What is a namespace in programming?
questions.correct_answer: A container that groups identifiers to avoid naming conflicts
incorrect_answers: The memory address of a variable
A protocol for naming network devices

questions.question: What is static memory allocation?
questions.correct_answer: Memory allocated at compile time with a fixed size that cannot change at runtime
incorrect_answers: Memory that persists after the program exits

questions.question: What does HTTP status code 500 indicate?
questions.correct_answer: Internal Server Error — something went wrong on the server

questions.question: What is the Factory design pattern?
questions.correct_answer: A creational pattern that provides an interface for creating objects without specifying the exact class
incorrect_answers: A pattern that restricts a class to one instance
A pattern that adds behavior to an object dynamically
A pattern that translates one interface into another
